title: Non-equilibrium glassy arrest and discontinuous transitions at avoided quantum critical points
abstract: The non-equilibrium dynamics of an order parameter confined by an external constraint field are investigated within a spatially extended Belitz-Kirkpatrick-Vojta framework. A generic non-monotonic dissipation peak arises at avoided criticality due to the interplay between macroscopic free-energy flattening and microscopic disorder-induced trapping. Near this regime, suppressed deterministic forces enhance activated trapping, leading to an effective violation of the fluctuation-dissipation theorem and ultimate glassy arrest. At higher fields, the system undergoes a discontinuous phase transition that bypasses this flat free-energy region. These explicit analytical scalings establish a generic mechanism for non-equilibrium arrest in disordered condensed matter systems.
summary: This theoretical work models non-equilibrium dynamics in disordered systems near avoided quantum critical points using a generalized Ginzburg-Landau framework. It demonstrates that glassy arrest arises from disorder-induced trapping when the system's free energy flattens. Conversely, strong external fields can force the system into a rapid, discontinuous phase transition, bypassing the glassy state.
main_problem: The paper investigates the non-equilibrium dynamics of order parameters subjected to external constraints, focusing on the mechanisms driving glassy arrest and discontinuous phase transitions near avoided quantum critical points.
main_result: It establishes a generic mechanism showing that glassy arrest occurs due to disorder-induced trapping when the macroscopic free energy flattens, while high external fields can induce a thermodynamically efficient discontinuous jump that bypasses this metastable region.
method: The analysis uses a continuum field theory approach, modeling dynamics via a stochastic Ginzburg-Landau equation, and analyzing non-equilibrium observables like dissipation and persistence probability.
model_or_system: The system is modeled using a spatially extended Belitz-Kirkpatrick-Vojta framework for an order parameter $\phi(\mathbf{r}, t)$, incorporating a macroscopic Landau-Ginzburg free energy functional and a quenched disorder potential.
key_observables: Dissipation peak, violation of the fluctuation-dissipation theorem (FDT), total dissipated energy ($\Delta E_{diss}$), and phase persistence probability.
important_parameters: External constraint field ($R_A$), disorder strength ($K$), and the effective temperature/noise scale ($T_{eff}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes adiabatic decoupling, where local fluctuations relax much faster than the global energy reservoir depletion ($	au_{fluct} \ll 	au_{depletion}$).
figures_summary: Figures visualize the constraint-induced free energy landscape, map phase persistence probability showing sharp drops at avoided critical thresholds, and track non-equilibrium kinetic trajectories under varying fields.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by defining the complex free energy landscape, analyzing the system's response in different field regimes (linear, avoided critical, high field), and deriving scaling relations for dissipation and arrest mechanisms.
why_interesting: The concepts of non-equilibrium dynamics, FDT violation, and glassy arrest are central to understanding open quantum systems and many-body relaxation processes, making it relevant for AMO physics and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Phenomenological visualization of the constraint-induced free energy landscape F across the primary (ϕ) and secondary (ψ) order parameters. (a) Top panel, Strong Field Regime (RA = 15): Under an intense external con- straint field, the macroscopic gradient forces the order parameter trajectory to- ward an orthogonal minimum. (b) Bottom panel, Zero Field Regime (RA = 0): Upon removal of the external field, emergent high-frequency quenched barri- ers block the return trajectory. Lacking sufficient thermal activation (Teff) to overcome these microscopic traps, the system undergoes non-equilibrium glassy arrest.

Figure 2: Persistence probability phase diagrams across active noise (Tstrat) and external constraint field intensity (RA). A U-shaped region of phase instability forms near the critical threshold. This trap deepens and widens significantly as quenched microscopic ruggedness (K) increases, dominating the macroscopic free-energy flattening and increasing the density of metastable states (configu- rational entropy).

Figure 3: Non-equilibrium kinetic trajectories of the order parameter field. Left panel: First-order discontinuous trajectories of the effective scalar field under varying constraint fields. Middle panel: Corresponding strain energy and resource depletion of the finite dissipative bath (RB). Right panel: Divergence of relaxation time precisely at the avoided critical threshold (RA ≈10). This critical slowing down is driven by the pinning of the spatial correlation length against localized quenched traps.

Figure 4: Thermodynamic signature and phase stability. Left panel: Diver- gence of the average entropy production rate (⟨˙ΣB⟩). Middle panel: Total dissipated energy (∆Ediss). The cost peaks non-monotonically precisely during the glassy arrest regime, while the first-order discontinuous jump (RA > 20) bypasses the flat free-energy region entirely. Right panel: Persistence prob- ability mapping the characteristic U-shaped region of phase instability caused by disorder-induced metastability.
